data.title: Centene: Temporary Headwinds, Guidance Steady
data.description:
Investment thesis

At first glance, Centene (NYSE:CNC) doesn’t seem like an obvious investment opportunity: due to an unexpected quarterly loss and strong industry headwinds, its share price dropped by a staggering 54%. But scratch beneath the surface and you get a different picture. A solid balance sheet, good profitability, and a growing managed care market instantly make the company look more interesting. Add in that said headwinds seem very much temporary rather than structural and that the management team is making concrete efforts to restore margins, and we have, in my opinion, a company that appears to be deeply undervalued. I therefore recommend a "strong buy."

Company information

Centene Corporation is a healthcare company that provides programs and services to underinsured and uninsured families, and commercial organizations in the U.S. According to the 10-K report, the company conducts its business via four segments. CNC's fiscal year ends on December 31.

The company is one of the largest players in the industry, with a TTM revenue of around $160 billion. Despite the temporary weakness [which we will discuss in detail later] in recent financial performance, CNC's average growth rates across all key P&L lines over the last decade are impressive.

In addition to impressive growth across key metrics over the last decade, CNC also boasts an "A-" profitability grade from Seeking Alpha Quant.

Temporary headwinds

I see three key reasons behind the sharp decline in Centene’s share price over the last year.

First up is the company’s surprise loss in Q2 of this year, triggered by a downward adjustment in ACA risk transfer revenues. Following a new actuarial analysis, the company had to lower its expected annual revenue by $1.8–$2.4 billion. There’s no doubt that this adjustment hit the top line hard and brewed uncertainty around the near-term outlook.

Secondly, medical costs have been surging across Medicaid and ACA populations. Medical loss ratios [MLR], or health benefit ratios [HBR], have been rising due to increased use of behavioral health services, home care, and high-cost drugs, especially in markets such as Florida and New York. Both are areas where Centene has a significant presence. This causes the market to shrink, making it harder for the company to reach its original targets set for 2025.

Next, the management withdrew its 2025 earnings guidance. This was due to market volatility, high morbidity in new enrollees, and regulatory changes, which in turn made claims patterns look unpredictable. Therefore, Centene decided it couldn’t provide reliable forecasts, a decision that was not well received by investors. When it comes to health insurance, clarity and consistency are key for investors.

Finally, I believe that the above headwinds have been further heightened by events in the broader market; a sector rotation out of managed care stocks has put pressure on government-focused insurers, like Centene, sending share prices plunging. However, all these headwinds appear to be more temporary than structural.

Structural strengths

What’s interesting is that Centene's adjusted diluted EPS for the full-year 2025 is still forecast to remain positive, with consensus estimates in the $1.21–$1.76 range, and is a sign that the company is expected to remain profitable. Being able to stand firm in wobbly times is a testament to a strong business model, in my opinion, and therefore shouldn’t be overlooked.

Another positive factor is the balance sheet. This shows a recurring premium revenue and a manageable debt-to-equity ratio of approximately 0.64. Cash flow from operations reached $1.8 billion in Q2 2025, which means the company is able to generate liquidity and financial stability. In short, the company has predictable cash inflows even during periods of high medical costs.

Centene’s CEO and management team are now fully focusing on profitability over growth. The company is also recalibrating risk, and repricing the 2026 ACA and Medicaid contracts, which should go a long way to restore its earnings power and predictability.

The company is also taking big strides to get its business in shape by restructuring business lines and geographies wherever the margins are unsustainable. By actively working with state partners, the company is also targeting a composite 5% upwards adjustment in 2025 for Medicaid rates. These negotiations are critical as Medicaid represents a large revenue share for the company, and any rate improvements will offset the rising medical costs.

Another important point is that Centene is in the process of implementing digital transformation initiatives to counteract fraud and streamline eligibility verification, and in doing so, laying some pretty strong foundations for sustained operational leverage.

A number of positive industry developments support Centene as a decent investment opportunity. Firstly, Centene is not the only company to be facing an uphill struggle. Warren Buffett recently started investing in UnitedHealth and experienced many of the same problems as Centene, confirming them as temporary rather than permanent. His move also proves that managed care remains a fundamentally attractive industry to invest in.

Another sign of the temporary nature of current headwinds is that yesterday Centene’s management reiterated their previous EPS guidance, meaning that the worst in terms of downgrades is behind us. Finally, the long-term secular picture looks positive; the U.S. population is rapidly aging, providing a large target market in the future.

As a result of guidance reiteration, CNC gained almost 9% intraday on September 11. As the technical setup suggests, the share price is currently notably higher than its 10D and 50D simple moving averages [SMA], and the gap with a 100D SMA is rapidly narrowing. In my opinion, such a pattern might signal the early stages of a trend change, where buyers are stepping in and pushing the price higher after a period of weakness.

Valuation

Let’s look at current prices: Centene is trading at a remarkable discount compared to its peers. Its GAAP P/E multiple is below 8, while its P/B ratio is around 0.6x, numbers that indicate that the market is valuing the company as though it were in structural decline, which, as we can see, isn’t the case.

Centene's TTM price/FCF ratio is below 9, while the historical average is around 18, meaning there is a 50% undervaluation gap. Forward P/E ratios are also extremely low, taking into account that a relatively rapid recovery of CNC's EPS is highly likely.

The historical average [5Y] TTM adjusted P/E ratio of CNC is 12.07. An average estimate of 12 Wall Street analysts for the FY2027 EPS is $4.71. The sample of analysts looks representative. With that being said, multiplying this EPS estimate by the historical average P/E is a reliable approach to calculate the target price for the end of FY2026. That said, $4.71 multiplied by 12.07 equals approximately $57. This value is 67% higher compared to the last close of around $34. The upside potential looks compelling, no doubt.

Risks to consider

I believe investors would need to take the following risks into account:

Firstly, medical cost inflation is unpredictable, especially from post-pandemic shifts in Medicaid and ACA, and is therefore a sector risk worth considering. Secondly, Centene has to deliver on its plans to restore margins by repricing contracts and securing rate adjustments. Failure to do this would undermine the investment case.

I should also mention policy risk. Centene relies heavily on state and federal programs, and if reimbursement rate requests are denied, or regulatory changes end up reducing funding, it will impact profits.

Bottom line

A 54% drop in share price this year doesn’t seem so scary after all, a sign of turbulence rather than permanent damage. Yes, the company is currently facing challenges, but it is still making money and has a strong balance sheet. Profits are good, revealing that the company is able to weather the storm. The management team is also intent on making the right changes, both digitally and towards restoring margins. Finally, with an aging population and an ongoing need for managed care, I see the company poised for recovery in 2026. I believe it’s a sound investment opportunity and recommend a "strong buy."

data.title: Why The Fiscal Deficit Won't Necessarily Lead To High Interest Rates
data.description:
Written by Austin Rogers for High Yield Investor.

There is always something to worry about.

Since the stock market came into existence, there has been a continuous parade of wars, recessions, depressions, bank runs, crashes, pandemics, stagflations, regime changes, high interest rates, negative interest rates, and about 700 Marvel movies.

Through it all, humanity has survived and continued to advance. Publicly traded companies, including REITs, have been resilient and proven themselves capable of growth and innovation no matter what.

Another apparently unstoppable force that has been at play for centuries is the national debt.

Since the 1970s, the U.S. government has almost continuously run a fiscal deficit, interrupted briefly by a period of fiscal surplus in the early 2000s as a result of capital gains from the Dot Com bubble. And for decades, people have worried about this endless ballooning of the national debt.

Almost everyone would insist that this cannot go on forever. And yet, no one can say how long it can go on. And, perhaps more critically, no one can predict how exactly it would end if it was brought to a halt.

A prevailing view is that the U.S. will lose its global reserve currency status, foreigners won't want to buy our Treasuries anymore, interest rates will surge higher, and the American economy will suffer a severe and prolonged (perhaps indefinite) contraction.

Another point that proponents of this view would note is that the U.S. has enjoyed reserve currency status for about the average length of time that previous reserve currencies enjoyed during their own respective heydays.

This pessimistic view was lent a boost from none other than Warren Buffett, the Oracle of Omaha, during the most recent Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) annual meeting when he said:

We’re operating at a fiscal deficit now that is unsustainable over a very long period of time. We don’t know whether that means two years or 20 years, because there’s never been a country like the United States. But you know, this is something that can’t go on forever . . . and it has the aspect to it that it gets uncontrollable at a certain point.

These bearish fiscal hawks might point to the recent decline of the US dollar (against other global currencies) as evidence that this process has already begun.

We doubt that.

It is true that the USD (DXY) has fallen precipitously this year, but compared to its range from the last 30 years, it is actually still around average or somewhat elevated.

Although the recent decline in the USD has been widely discussed, it has not been widely noted that even after the decline, the USD has basically returned to its pre-COVID level.

But what about the incredible surge in the price of gold (XAUUSD:CUR) in recent years? Could this be a sign that gold is gradually replacing the USD as a reserve asset?

While it is true that many central banks around the world have been accumulating more gold to hold in reserve, this is not really a sign that the dollar is losing its reserve status. Contracts and loans still have to be denominated in an easily transferable medium of exchange. Physical gold is not easily divisible or transferable. It would require some trusted (and costly) third party to carry out international settlements.

Plus, there is a natural ceiling to the level that governments and central banks can accumulate gold reserves, because the larger the reserve, the greater the nation's vulnerability to fluctuations in the price of gold.

Lastly, it should be noted that there is no meaningful movement anywhere in the world to return to a gold standard, which further eliminates gold as a real threat to the USD's reserve supremacy.

Rather, the price of gold is largely driven by sentiment, often driven to new highs when government debt-to-GDP is surging higher (as is happening today).

Those who claim the U.S. is on the verge of losing global reserve currency leadership might also point to the foreign ownership of U.S. Treasury debt as a share of the total, which has declined from over 50% during the Great Financial Crisis to about 1/3rd today.

This certainly looks concerning, but you will notice that there are ebbs and flows to foreigners' share of Treasury debt over time. For example, it spiked in the late 1970s only to fall in the early 1980s. It spiked in the late 1990s only to fall in the early 2000s.

The truth is that foreigners have continued to buy U.S. government debt. They've been buying a lot of it. Since the beginning of 2019, foreigners' collective holdings of U.S. Treasuries have increased by about $2.5 trillion.

Note that foreigners did not abandon U.S. Treasuries even during 2020 and 2021, when pandemic-era fiscal stimulus resulted in a flood of new U.S. government debt entering the market.

Since 2012, the Chinese have been shedding their Treasury holdings, but the Europeans have more than offset Chinese selling.

As a matter of fact, the Canadians also expanded their Treasury holdings by almost the same amount as the Chinese shrank theirs from 2012 through the end of 2024. But in 2025, Canadians collectively dumped Treasuries to divest of U.S. assets in protest against the tariffs.

Anyway, the biggest reason why the foreigner share of Treasury ownership has fallen since the GFC is that (1) the US government has been issuing debt at a faster pace and (2) Americans have increased their share of Treasury ownership.

US households, banks, insurance companies, pension funds, mutual funds, money market funds, and state & local governments have all meaningfully increased their Treasury ownership since the GFC.

Can Americans collectively keep absorbing an expanding amount of Treasury issuance?

While the fiscal deficit was gradually expanding even before COVID-19, the pandemic blew out the deficit to a recent level of about $2 trillion per year.

If the U.S. government continues down this path of deficit spending, the country's debt-to-GDP ratio will continue to climb higher and higher:

Unlike the WWII period, which was an acute driver of government debt accumulation, the current trajectory of debt-to-GDP is secular and structural.

So, again, if foreign buying of Treasuries doesn't keep up with the pace of issuance, then can Americans absorb the balance of government debt issuance without interest rates soaring higher?

Yes, we think so.

Consider this: To finance deficit spending, the government issues debt. This involves a two-way flow of money. Someone receives the proceeds of government spending, and someone else provides the capital to fund that spending.

Money from the public is sapped to fund deficit spending, but the deficit spending also sends money out into the public.

That government spending filters through the economy and eventually into the coffers of banks, insurance companies, pension funds, affluent households, or state & local governments that then use at least some of it to buy Treasuries.

In other words, the government's deficit spending increases the savings of the private sector and state & local governments, which can then be used to finance more deficit spending.

If the economy was running out of savings with which to finance deficit spending, then we would see this show up in higher interest rates. But that is not what we observe when comparing deficits to interest rates.

In fact, there is little to no correlation between the general level of deficit spending and changes in interest rates over time.

During the early 1980s, interest rates were extraordinarily high at the same time as debt-to-GDP hovered around the low single-digits.

Around 2000, during the peak of the Dot-Com bubble when the U.S. government actually began to run a fiscal surplus, interest rates were actually rising.

Over multi-year periods of time, there simply is no meaningful correlation between the level of deficit spending and the level of interest rates.

Instead, interest rates are overwhelmingly determined by two factors: The Federal Reserve and nominal GDP.

The Fed holds heavy sway over short-term interest rates through their control of the overnight/inter-bank rate, whereas long-term interest rates basically track with nominal GDP growth -- real GDP growth plus inflation.

When the market thinks that real GDP growth and/or inflation are going higher, long-term interest rates rise.

When the market thinks that real GDP growth and/or inflation are going lower, long-term interest rates fall.

While the current situation plays a major role here, it is often the market's expectations for the near future that play an even larger role.

For example, nominal GDP growth topped 10% in 2021, but the 10-year Treasury rate never rose past 5%, because the market knew that this incredible 10% nominal GDP growth rate would only be temporary.

As we write this, the 10-year Treasury rate (US10Y) is right around 4%, while the Fed's projections for 2025 put real GDP growth at 1.4% and inflation at 3.0%. Combine those two and you get a nominal GDP growth rate of... 4.4%.

For 2026, the Fed projects nominal GDP growth of 4.0%, and in 2027, the Fed forecasts 3.9% growth.

Therefore, even as the U.S. government continues to run a significant fiscal deficit, long-term interest rates should gradually come down over the next few years (assuming the Fed's economic projections are more or less accurate).

The Real Crowd-Out Effect

The traditional "crowd-out" theory of government deficit spending posits that federal government borrowing competes with private sector and state/local borrowing for a limited pool of capital, and thus, when the federal government increases its deficit spending, it incrementally crowds out other borrowers, pushing up interest rates.

But there are two reasons we aren't very worried about the traditional crowd-out effect.

First, as we argued above, deficit spending ultimately increases the pool of capital from which government debt issuance can be financed.

Second, U.S. private sector debt to GDP has declined since the GFC as households and businesses have collectively deleveraged since that painful recessionary period.

For example, here's U.S. household debt to GDP:

Household debt to GDP, which includes mortgages but also various forms of consumer debt, has fallen from 100% during the GFC to the low 70% area today.

Here's a chart from 2005 to the present, illustrating each nonfinancial sector's share of total debt in the U.S.:

As you can see, since 2005, household and state/local debt have both shrunk as a share of the total, while business debt accounts for about the same ~30% of the total.

In 2008, the business sector's debt-to-GDP ratio stood at 72.5%. In 2025, it was 72.6%. Household debt-to-GDP was 98% in 2008 and 67.6% in 2025. State & local debt-to-GDP was 20.2% in 2008 and 11.4% in 2025.

But federal government debt has dramatically increased its share of total debt.

In 2008, federal government debt-to-GDP stood at 42.5%, and by 2025 it had climbed to 106%.

This isn't a sign of the crowd-out effect, because interest rates have not been continuously rising since the GFC. Rather, it is merely an illustration of the way in which the federal government has levered up at the same time as the private sector has deleveraged.

But while we are not terribly concerned about the traditional crowd-out effect, that does not mean there are no negative consequences to sustained deficit spending.

According to the consensus of economic research, there is a crowd-out effect of sustained deficit spending, just not the traditional one.

In fact, there are really two forms of crowding out that come from sustained and/or increasing deficit spending:

  1. There is a crowd-out effect within the government's own budget. As interest expense and other forms of spending with low fiscal multipliers increase, the share of the budget allocated to discretionary spending on economy-growing investments are crowded out and shrink.
  2. As the government spends more, it commands more of the nation's limited physical and human capital, pulling resources from the more productive part of the economy to the less productive part of the economy.

While financial capital is effectively unlimited, physical and human capital most certainly are limited.

As the government spends more and the structural fiscal deficit grows, more of a society's scarce resources such as physical production capacity and human workers get allocated to certain tasks according to the government's priorities. But those activities, on average, are less productive and economy-growing than they would be if allocated by the private sector.

(We are not saying that all government jobs are unproductive or unnecessary. We're saying that the average U.S. government job is less productive than the average private sector job, and the average dollar of government-allocated capital is less productive than the average dollar of market-allocated capital.)

It isn't that money becomes more scarce and expensive for private sector investment as a result of deficit spending. It's that real resources like people and infrastructure do.

It isn't that deficit spending saps financial capital from private investment. It's that fewer people and real resources are being directed toward private investment because more of them are being directed toward government activities.

The cumulative effect of this unproductive (or at least sub-optimal) allocation of a country's resources is a rising national debt-to-GDP ratio and declining real GDP growth.

That is a major reason (along with falling population growth) why the Congressional Budget Office forecasts slower and slower real GDP growth over the next 30 years:

As you can see, the CBO projects falling population growth to have a larger negative impact on real GDP growth than the lower labor force productivity resulting from sustained fiscal deficits. All else being equal, we would assume that productivity growth would fall even faster. But AI and automation could offset a lot of negative effects of fiscal deficits.

Holding inflation constant, declining real GDP growth (partially as a result of deficit spending and the mounting national debt) should translate into lower long-term interest rates.

This relationship isn't new. It has been evident for decades now.

As nominal GDP growth has declined, so too have long-term interest rates.

As nominal GDP growth continues to decline, so too should long-term interest rates.

That is why even a sustained fiscal deficit will not necessarily lead to higher interest rates.

And, as we have been saying for a while now, dividend stocks should be major winners from a further decline in long-term interest rates. For that reason (among others), we believe our portfolios at High Yield Investor are well-positioned to outperform going forward.

data.title: How Large Can Palantir Grow
data.description:
AI was a game-changer for many firms.

That's why last week, I've written an article, "How Large Can Nvidia (NVDA) Grow - It Won't 10x Anymore". Today I aim to look at Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR) through a similar scope. Answering the burning question.

Palantir catch attention of many. Including me. I've criticized it for its secretive practices, positioned as government tech defense contractor. The firm proved me wrong.

Palantir completely changed the game and its revenue distribution. 55% revenue is still generated via government, but it's commercial segment is growing at unprecedented speed, and soon will take over.

The firm's shares are up 20x in last three years. It's now 23rd most valuable firm in US with a market cap approaching $400B.

Palantir's Business

Palantir's business is split into two main segments:

  • Government: best-known for its tech-related work with US military and intel agencies (NSA, CIA, FBI). A great and sticky business, but it wouldn't scale.
  • Commercial: Palantir's future - focusing on enterprise-grade data solutions, allowing firms to combine different data sources, visualize them, and ultimately allow higher-quality decision making.

But it's 55% government-focused and 45% commercial-focused scope is changing. Palantir AI platform is starting to become a standalone product, which will define PLTR's future. AIP-as its often referred to-is scalable, easy to configure platform aimed at the integration of customer's data, in combination with LLMs, while meeting security standards. The best part? Deployment of configured AIP to client won't take more than days.

Adoption of new tools is often a challenge. And, firms generally don't want to change processes that work well. To address this, Palantir launched AIP-oriented "bootcamps", where PLTR showcases to their potential clients the platform, using the clients' data. This strategy proved extremely successful, as AI is novel technology, and it isn't always easy to imagine what it can do. Visual presentation is game-changer.

That's the reason why in Q2 Palantir closed 157 deals of at least $1M+ and within that, 42 $10M+.

In Q2, Palantir posted 8th consecutive quarter of revenue growth. Combined revenue grew 48% YoY and 14% QoQ (QoQ now is similar to rate Nvidia was growing between 2023-2025). US revenue alone grew 68% YoY. The quarterly revenue for the first time reached $1B in Q2, meaning without any growth (which is hard to imagine) Palantir is now $4B annual run rate revenue firm.

If we were to dissect further, to put into context why I am suggesting Commercial segment will be the growth driver, is because this segment in US alone grew 93% YoY. Revenue generated via government grew 53%. This gives you a good idea, that in 2-4 quarters, Commercial will take over. And that's mostly US. Many opportunities are awaiting Palantir in international waters.

What's better? The firm is highly profitable. It's operating margin in Q2 was 16.5% (and, improving). And, GAAP EPS landed at $0.13/share.

Valuation

The issue? As always - valuation.

On a trailing-twelve-month basis it's priced at 112.3x price-to-sales. A P/E of 315.

You think forward valuation is better? Yes, but its 12M-forward P/E is still at 260x.

No surprise. It appears all is going well for Palantir - massive growth, profitable business, huge addressable market, AI tailwinds, lack of competition and S&P 500 (SP500) admission.

But, as you can see from the chart below, which compares market cap. to revenue growth, Palantir's valuation far outperformed it's top-line growth. You see where I am heading with this - most of the growth comes from valuation expansion rather than fundamentals.

It's time to ask the question, whether the re-rating is deserved? I would say yes, to certain degree. Because, we cannot base fundamentals only on revenue/EPS growth. There are other facts at play, such as the size of the addressable market, the lack of competition as I've already mentioned, etc...

But, we certainly shouldn't assume this will continue forever. The valuation will need to contract - it will become a headwind. To avoid stock decline, the fundamentals will need grow fast enough to offset the decline in valuation. It's hard to imagine, $1T stock trading at 100x sales.

Palantir is pure AI/Software play. It doesn't have direct peers (its product offering is novel), but we could take businesses like Adobe (ADBE), ServiceNow (NOW), Intuit (INTU) or Salesforce (CRM) as a gauge where it's valuation stand:

It doesn't need much explanation. 5x more expensive than the 2nd most expensive - ServiceNow.

We could argue PLTR's revenue run-rate (≈$4B) is smaller compared to all the firms I've selected, which all bring in at least $12B+ annually. But it isn't smaller by market cap - due to valuation. Though, the smaller revenue run-rate unlocks more opportunities.

Palantir's Path Into The Future

First, let's recap a bit:

  • Palantir's market cap currently is $400B.
  • Current annual revenue run-rate is ≈$4B.
  • From 2018 - 2025 (assuming $4B revenue delivery this year) revenue grew at annualized rate of 36.5%.
  • Price-to-Sales (P/S) is 112x.

We already know now, the revenue growth accelerated, in last quarter being 48%. I do think we can reasonable expect at least 40% annual revenue growth in the next 2-3 years.

So, the first question is, what needs to happen for Palantir to hit $1T market cap? - 2.5x growth (excl. stock dilution)

If we would retain elevated P/S at 100 and assume 40% revenue growth, Palantir's revenue run rate would be $10B/annum and market cap would reach $1T in 3.07 year. This is very bullish scenario, because as revenue run rate increases, we would expect P/S contraction.

A more realistic scenario, in my view, is P/S at 50 and growth at 40%. In this instance, Palantir's would need to have $20B/annum revenue run-rate. Underneath this assumptions a $1T market cap would be reached in 5.13 years.

You see, I am not questioning the revenue growth. I expect this shouldn't be a problem, particularly as Palantir accelerates its expansion outside of US. What I am questioning is where the valuation should sits, as this has meaningful implications on its future growth. As I said previously, this will be future headwind.

Reaching $1T is major leap. In revenue, profit margins, scale of business and successful execution. Few of tech firms, reached this milestone.

Can Palantir do it? Absolutely. Commercial expansion - in US and outside, new US military contracts, NATO's Maven Smart System, are all levers how to pull this off. I think on the execution side, Palantir proven it can deliver, well-above expectations. Where I see risk is economy. If economy starts to weakening, as we see with just 22k job additions in August, FED being behind the curve with late rate cuts, or inflation remains sticky with latest report at 2.9%, CAPEX spending could drop, negative impacting PLTR's plans. A macro backdrop which cannot be influenced.

But, what about long-term?

The real question is what is the size of addressable market. Palantir doesn't disclose this figure and estimates are all over the place. It depends on what we consider its market is. If we consider Big Data/Analytics + AI software, the market's size now is est. around $500-800B. If we account for AI growth, it could be $1.2-2T. As you see, all over the place.

It's fair to say, that even if the market isn't limitation for now, at some point it will be. Palantir won't grow it's revenue at 40% annualized rate infinitely. I am expecting:

  • 40% annualized growth until 2028
  • 30% annualized growth in 2029 - 2031
  • 20% annualized growth in 2032 - 2033

... by the end of 2033 Palantir's potential revenue could be $35B.

The market cap will be following at different P/S:

  • At P/S of 25x = $875B
  • At P/S of 30x = $1.1T
  • At P/S of 40x = $1.4T
  • At P/S of 50x = $1.75T

Ultimately, your ROR will depend on the valuation's path forward.

Investor's Takeaway

Palantir is a millionaire-maker stock. Up 20x in last 3 years alone.

I've missed it. Unfortunately, but that's investing.

I think with Palantir the questions isn't whether it will grow, but rather whether at 30,40,50% annually? And, even more important, what will be the future valuation?

The firm deserves a premium valuation, but it's the most expensive stock in my coverage. The firm needs to execute flawlessly, scale, keep increasing its margins, retain customers and enter new markets. For now, there isn't competition, but that may change.

I expect Palantir can become $875B - $1.75T market cap firm by end of 2033.

data.title: Sun Hung Kai Properties: A Buy On Earnings Turnaround And Potential Catalysts
data.description:
I'm a bull on Sun Hung Kai Properties Limited (OTCPK:SUHJY) (OTCPK:SUHJF) (0016.HK).

SUHJY's bottom line contracted for three years (FY2022-2024) in a row following my prior June 18, 2021, write-up.

The firm has finally reached an inflection point with its FY2025 (YE Jun) results. I've also identified a number of new re-rating triggers.

Net Profit Expansion Resumes

Last week, SUHJY issued its full-year financial announcement for July 1, 2024-June 30, 2025.

Its FY25 normalized earnings went up +1% to HK$21.9B, which I find encouraging.

The group's net income declined by -4%, -17%, -9% in FY22, FY23, and FY24, respectively. SUHJY had been hurt by a heavy debt burden and weak demand in the past. These are less of a concern now.

Net debt-to-equity for the company rose from 0.17 times at the end of FY2022 to 0.21x as of December 31, 2023. This was a 10-year high coinciding with FY23's bottom line drop in the teens.

SUHJY's gearing has come down to a more comfortable 15% by the end of FY25. At the analyst briefing, it stressed that "the group will continue its prudent financial management."

Its cost of debt also decreased -0.7 ppts (percentage points) to 3.7% in the recent fiscal year. During the same time frame, the 3M "Hong Kong Interbank Offered Rate," or "HIBOR," a proxy for Hong Kong or HK corporates' borrowing rates, was 310bps (basis points) lower.

SUHJY saw its FY2025 net interest expense fall 30% to HK$2.5B, thanks to deleveraging efforts and a more accommodating rate environment.

Separately, its property development EBIT grew +5% in the latest full-year period.

SUHJY's results release noted the outperformance was "mostly driven by profit from the sales of residential units in Shanghai Arch Phase 3", which "received high acclaim for their stunning views of the Bund." Travel website MakeMyTrip describes "The Bund" as "a famous waterfront area in central Shanghai, offering stunning views of the city's skyline and historic architecture."

It's all about location when you are choosing your dream home. I can understand why SUHJY's "Shanghai Arch" project has been selling well.

My view is that the stock can continue to register higher net income going forward.

I'm projecting a 20% improvement in net finance costs for the next fiscal year. As mentioned above, the enterprise has the intent to maintain a strong balance sheet, and rates are on a downtrend.

SUHJY also shared at its results call it will roll out the "last phase" of "Shanghai Arch" soon, which is referred to as "villas" that are "rare" in this part of the city. I believe that the favorable momentum from "Phase 3" can be sustained with the new launch.

My forecast is an earnings growth acceleration from FY25's 1% to FY26's +5%.

I'm Watching For Multiple Catalysts

There are future developments that could push up its share price.

SUHJY's FY2025 performance could have been even better. Its HK property segment's "Development Property" or "DP" margin more than halved from 26% for FY24 to 12% in the recent fiscal year.

It participated in HSBC Holdings' (HSBC) "Non-Deal Roadshow" this week. The bank published a report (not publicly available) titled "Key Takeaways From NDR" on September 5 detailing management's disclosures. SUHJY indicated that "the (HK) housing market has displayed good signs of stabilization amid a lower local mortgage rate." It's anticipating "better selling prices in the subsequent phases" of certain local projects.

I think we might be past the worst of Hong Kong's real estate downturn, judging by local rate trends and the company's remarks. A possible rebound in this division's profitability should be well-received by investors.

Also, HK's Chief Executive is making his annual policy address on September 17. I'm expecting favorable supportive measures for the domestic property sector.

Last year's event had the "loan-to-value ratio for home mortgages relaxed," according to the media. My take is that stamp duty reduction and a lower investment threshold to qualify for local residency are potential initiatives that the HK government might introduce.

There are also opportunities for SUHJY to become more investor-friendly.

The firm committed to distributing half of its future net profit at its FY25 results meeting. The earlier policy was a slightly inferior "40%-50%" payout.

SUHJY has never done any repurchases in its history. Its five-year mean dividend-to-earnings ratio was also higher at 52%.

I won't rule out the possibility of the group doing dividend hikes or a maiden purchase of its own shares. The stock's trailing shareholder yield is a decent 3.9% with room for further upside in the future.

You Should Consider These Risks

I would be worried, if SUHJY takes on substantial new borrowings and interest rates rise again.

Assuming that the key catalysts like improved stockholder returns and policy tailwinds aren't realized, this could disappoint the investing community.

My Concluding Thoughts

I rate SUHJY as a Buy.

The market currently values it at 0.45x P/B.

The stock was trading at 0.47-0.63x book (source: S&P Capital IQ) in FY21 when its bottom-line growth was positive.

My target P/NAV for SUHJY is 0.55x (FY2021's midpoint), implying an upside of +22% (0.55/0.45).

Its earnings have turned around, and there are visible catalysts supporting share price appreciation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SentinelOne: 3 Reasons Why I'm Not Buying
data.description:
SentinelOne (NYSE:S) has underperformed the market this year, with its YTD performance coming in at -19% compared to +12% for the S&P 500 (SPX). Not to mention, the stock hasn't done much for the past three years, roughly. This has left its valuation at a relatively low level on a price/sales basis compared to peers like CrowdStrike (CRWD), which has gained 26% this year. At the same time, the stock is putting in higher lows and forming a multi-year bottom after a long downtrend.

Should you buy? While the chart currently suggests that downside pressure is limited for now (unless the chart breaks down), the problem is that the fundamentals aren't as great as I'd like them to be. So, buying may not be a horrible idea, if you're looking for a technical play, but I prefer to stay out of this, giving it a Hold rating instead.

When it comes to the fundamentals, I have three main concerns, which I've bolded below. Despite encouraging updates in the Q2 earnings call (like improving margins, raised revenue guidance, the reaffirmation of positive free cash flow guidance, and continued high revenue growth), SentinelOne is still posting GAAP losses, relies on stock-based compensation, and has a Rule of 40 well under 40.

Also, based on analysts' estimates, the company's Rule of 40 metric should still be under 40 even two years from now, while competitors have much better numbers. Additionally, if you adjust for stock-based compensation, SentinelOne's FCF is still deeply negative.

The current ~7x forward P/S multiple for S stock is relatively cheap compared to some peers like CrowdStrike and Palo Alto Networks (PANW), but the company also isn't at the same level as them. For me to turn bullish, I'd want to see an improvement in fundamentals, as long as the chart remains constructive.

The Positives Of SentinelOne's Fundamentals

SentinelOne isn't an outright bad company. There are plenty of worse ones. Here are some of the things I like about SentinelOne.

Revenue Growth Rate Is Attractive, So Is The Gross Margin

In Q2, the firm's revenue grew by 22% year-over-year, and the same 22% growth rate is expected for the full fiscal year. Gross profitability is also strong, with a 75% GAAP gross profit margin that has been growing over time and is slightly higher than those of CrowdStrike (74.2%) and Palo Alto Networks (73.4%) despite having lower revenue.

Free Cash Flow Trend Is Encouraging

Another positive is that the company's free cash flow is now positive compared to prior years when it was negative.

Per analyst consensus, FCF is expected to continue growing in the next couple of years, all the way to $215.94 million by fiscal 2028, compared to $39.8 million in the past 12 months.

3 Reasons Why I'm Not Buying SentinelOne Stock

There are three reasons I'm assigning S stock a Hold rating instead of a Buy. As mentioned earlier, the reasons are high SBC relative to FCF, GAAP losses, and a Rule of 40 under 40. Let's get into it.

High Stock-Based Compensation Relative To FCF, GAAP Losses

The free cash flow trend shown above is good, but it still doesn't change the fact that FCF is negative if you subtract stock-based compensation, and it will likely still be that way two years from now.

In the past 12 months, stock-based compensation for SentinelOne has been $286.8 million, which means that free cash flow minus SBC is closer to -$247 million. Why subtract SBC from FCF? Because it gets added back to free cash flow in the cash flow statement, but it's a real expense, as it dilutes shareholders.

It's a more conservative measure, and the high SBC explains why non-GAAP earnings were positive in Q2 but GAAP earnings were negative.

Considering that SBC tends to trend higher over the years, it's likely that the projected $215.9 million in FCF for fiscal 2028 still won't be enough to cover SBC.

Side note: SentinelOne has never posted positive GAAP earnings, as you can see below. GAAP losses have actually been increasing.

Rule of 40 Under 40

You may be familiar with the Rule of 40, which is commonly used for SaaS companies, but if you're not, here's a quick definition. It's the sum of revenue growth and free cash flow margin (or operating margins; it can vary) of a company in a specified period. Example: 20% revenue growth and a 15% FCF margin will get you a "35%" score in the Rule of 40. It's called the Rule of 40 because investors generally look for a result of 40 or above to indicate great quality.

Unfortunately, SentinelOne used to have a score above 40%, but this figure has now fallen to 29.8% for the past 12 months.

And based on analysts' estimates, it doesn't seem like it will get above 40% in the next two years. For instance, revenue growth for fiscal 2028 is expected to be 18.35%, and the FCF margin (manually calculated, based on the analyst estimate screenshot earlier in the article) should be 15.1%. That would get you a score of ~33.45% -- still not enough.

Meanwhile, there are a few cybersecurity stocks that are already above 40%, including Fortinet (FTNT) at 46.5%, CrowdStrike at 49%, Check Point Software (CHKP) at 49.8%, Palo Alto Networks at 52.5%, and Zscaler (ZS) at 53.5%. I believe that speaks for itself.

Yes, The Low Valuation Prices Some Of This In

It can be argued that SentinelOne's price/sales ratio of ~7x makes it attractive compared to CrowdStrike and Palo Alto, with their P/S ratios of ~25x and 14.4x, respectively. Palo Alto is a much larger and more diversified company, while CrowdStrike is a more direct competitor, so let's look closer at that. Both companies have expected revenue growth rates near the 20% range for the next couple of years, and they have similar gross margins, so why the huge discrepancy?

I believe part of it has to do with CrowdStrike being bigger ($4.34 billion in TTM revenue compared to $907 million for SentinelOne), making it more "trustworthy," as well as the fact that CRWD has a much more proven free cash flow profile and has positive FCF even after subtracting SBC.

Another part of it probably has to do with hype, as I'm not even so sure whether CRWD should be trading at 25x in the first place. But when something is trending higher, investors pile on. Further, CrowdStrike is an S&P 500 company, which is a reason for a higher multiple. SentinelOne isn't set to join the S&P 500 anytime soon.

Nonetheless, even with a seemingly attractive P/S multiple, the concerns I have with the fundamentals keep me from buying right now.

The Chart Is Starting To Look Better

As mentioned earlier, S stock's chart looks like it's bottoming. Put simply, it's putting in higher lows and is near a rising trendline. That's why I'm not 100% against the idea of treating the stock as a technical trade, if that's your style. Stop losses can be placed under one of the recent lows (under $15.36 or under $14.33 if you want to give it more room).

However, I'd start to get worried if it broke under the trendline or recent support levels, especially if the stock does this while the overall market continues to be strong.

The Takeaway

SentinelOne stock is not the worst opportunity in the market, but maybe not the best, either. Positives include good revenue growth, strong gross profit margins, growing free cash flow, a relatively low P/S ratio, and a stock chart that looks to be putting in a bottom.

Still, negatives include high stock-based compensation relative to free cash flow, a Rule of 40 score under 40 (and this is set to continue), negative GAAP earnings, and a negative YTD performance while the market has been bullish overall.

A technical trade can work based on the chart, but if we're looking at the fundamentals only, it's not an A+ setup. I'd like to see the numbers improve before upgrading the stock to a buy. Thus, I believe S stock is a hold.

data.title: Corporate Earnings Slowdown Signaled By Employment Data
data.description:
The latest employment data strongly warned of a potential corporate earnings slowdown ahead. This is the first time we have warned about the employment data and its impact on corporate earnings. In May, we penned “Employment Data Confirms Economy Is Slowing." wherein we stated:

“Given the importance of consumption in the economy and that employment (production) must come first in the cycle, attention to employment data, particularly full-time employment, is crucial to determining economic risk. The risk of a recession remains very low; however, that can change if something causes consumption to contract quickly. Aside from an unexpected, exogenous impact, investors should expect economic growth to continue to slowly weaken to a longer-term trend slightly less than 2% annually. Unfortunately, while not recessionary, that growth rate will make it hard for corporate profitability to remain at record levels.”

The August 2025 employment report further confirmed the deceleration in job growth. Nonfarm payrolls added just 22,000 positions. Economists had expected over 75,000. June’s numbers were revised downward to a net loss of 13,000 jobs, the first monthly decline since 2020. July gained only a minor upward revision. However, what is most important is the “trend” of the data rather than just a one-month data point. As shown, the 3-month average of employment is deteriorating sharply, which has only occurred previously just before the onset of a recession.

While the labor force participation rate was 62.3 percent, still well below pre-COVID levels, the percentage of total full-time employees continues to drop sharply. That data point is essential because full-time employment is required to sustain economic growth. With that level well below pre-COVID levels, it is unsurprising that economic growth rates are slowing.

It isn’t just slower economic growth that the latest employment report suggests. Yes, growth is slowing because jobs are shrinking, and as a consequence, households are spending less. As we showed in a recent #BullBearReport, economic growth, inflation, and personal consumption are trending lower, given that employment, particularly full-time employment, supports economic supply and demand.

This data also confirms why the Fed is already behind the curve in cutting interest rates.

Why the Fed Is Likely Behind the Curve

Despite the slowdown, the Federal Reserve remains hesitant. Chair Powell noted softening labor conditions at Jackson Hole and suggested the door is open to rate cuts. But no concrete shift in policy has occurred. The Fed insists on being data-dependent while ignoring the most immediate data: labor markets are deteriorating. Inflation is not, and has not been, a threat, and the two-year Treasury yield, a close approximation of what the Fed funds rate should be, is already more than 80 bps lower than the Fed’s current policy rate.

The latest Beige Book also revealed soft hiring trends and growing caution among employers, with businesses pulling job postings and limiting expansion. That’s not a labor supply issue; it’s a demand issue. The Fed’s ongoing focus on lagging inflation data means that policies remain too tight and are now well “behind the curve.”

Markets aren’t waiting. Fed futures now imply near certainty of a September rate cut, with some traders pricing in 50 basis points. While many remain concerned about the risk of inflation, bond yields are already warning that the economic data is more disinflationary than not. As we have discussed before, despite all the “fear mongers” warning of surging interest rates, the reality is that interest rates on the long-end will track the economy. As we discussed in Grant: Rates Are Going Much Higher?”

“…let’s create a composite index of wages (which provides consumer purchasing power, aka demand), economic growth (the result of production and consumption), and inflation (the byproduct of increased demand from rising economic activity). We then compare that composite index to interest rates. Unsurprisingly, there is a high correlation between economic activity, inflation, and interest rates as rates respond to the drivers of inflation.”

We further discussed that relationship in “Tudor Jones: I Won’t Own Bonds.”

“The previous surge in inflation, and ultimately interest rates, was not a function of organic economic growth. It was a stimulus-driven surge in the supply/demand equation following the pandemic-driven shutdown. As those monetary and fiscal inflows reverse, that support will fade. In the future, we must understand the factors that drive rates over time: economic growth, wages, and inflation.”

With the economy and its primary driver, employment, slowing, the Fed’s delay in cutting rates increases the risk of a more substantial economic downturn. By maintaining rates at an elevated level, the negative impact on consumption (demand) is increasing. The Fed’s tightening has already filtered into credit, housing, and business spending. The labor market is reacting, and further policy lags will likely make the next easing cycle less effective.

The risk is that the central bank may be easing into a downturn it failed to prevent.

Implications for Corporate Earnings and Profit Margins

For investors, the most significant consequence is a slowdown in corporate earnings. A corporate earnings slowdown is already underway. Revenue growth is faltering, and companies, particularly in the retail and fast dining sectors, are seeing less pricing power as consumer demand slips. Eventually, those forces will compress profit margins. As we noted with respect to Q2 earnings:

“While technology and AI-driven firms have recently become bright spots, their strength cannot offset broader corporate margin pressures. In Q2, S&P 500 earnings grew 6.4%, with 80 percent of companies beating estimates. But this masks a weakening breadth of growth, where earnings beats are concentrated in essentially just two sectors. There would have been no earnings growth without Megacap Technology and major Wall Street banks.”

While many firms relied on price hikes, labor efficiency, and cost-cutting to drive earnings growth this year, that playbook is limited in scope and becoming less effective. While tech and AI-linked companies like Broadcom have offered bright spots, the rest of the market is under pressure. Discretionary sectors, cyclical industrials, and small-cap firms are more exposed to demand shocks and slowing economic growth. While investors are currently ignoring the linkages between economic demand and corporate earnings, as margins erode, the impact on earnings will become more significant.

Currently, analyst estimates still assume robust earnings growth into 2026. However, that will change in the months ahead. As those earnings estimates are revised lower, the risk to the market and currently very optimistic investors, is the question of valuations. As the corporate earnings slowdown accelerates, forward guidance will get cut, and paying significantly high multiples for earnings will be questioned. If companies revise down expectations, delay investments, and increase layoffs to protect bottom lines, the risk to markets will increase significantly.

Navigating The Risks

The evidence points to a slowing US economy. Growth is weakening, inflation remains elevated, corporate margins face pressure, and interest rate cuts are likely. These conditions require a shift in investment strategy. Investors must adapt to preserve capital, generate income, and manage risk. Positioning should emphasize resilience, quality, and income stability. The goal is to reduce exposure to volatile sectors and concentrate on assets that perform well during economic slowdowns.

Here are key actions investors should consider:

  • Reduce exposure to cyclical stocks: Cut back on discretionary sectors like retail, travel, and consumer electronics that rely heavily on strong economic growth.
  • Increase allocation to defensive sectors: Focus on consumer staples, healthcare, and utilities. These sectors provide stable earnings even in weak environments.
  • Favor companies with strong pricing power: These firms can better maintain margins despite rising input costs.
  • Prioritize strong balance sheets: Low debt and high cash reserves reduce financial stress and support consistent returns.
  • Add high-quality dividend payers: Look for companies with a track record of stable or growing dividends. These provide income support as capital gains slow.
  • Increase fixed income exposure: Short-duration bonds and high-grade corporates may benefit from falling interest rates.
  • Consider yield curve positioning: A steeper yield curve from rate cuts may create an opportunity in intermediate bonds.
  • Avoid speculative growth stocks. These firms rely on future earnings and cheap financing, both of which will be under pressure in a slowing economy.

A decelerating US economy changes the return profile across asset classes. Adjusting now to focus on quality, cash flow, and defensive positioning can improve downside protection and set the stage for more stable portfolio returns.

While there are no guarantees, the current gap between what Wall Street expects and what the economy can deliver is very different. Could the economy catch up to meet Wall Street’s expectations? Sure. It just usually doesn’t happen that way.

Most importantly, the Fed is late once again, and history suggests the impact on stocks will be negative.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Warner Bros Discovery: A Potential Bid Means The Hard Work Is Done
data.description:
Warner Bros Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD) stock highlights a frequent mispricing situation that the market often does. Roughly 3 years ago I wrote an article on John Malone's thoughts about the whole situation. He stated that he sold puts to support management. That put his cost if he ended up with the shares in the upper teens. He also stated that things were worse than management anticipated after the acquisition closed. These are things that Mr. Market wants solved quickly or just as quickly Mr. Market will punish the stock.

Last Article

The last article noted a big debt gain as part of the earnings report. Management has far outperformed in debt reduction even prior to that gain. That is about the only thing that happened quickly from Mr. Market's viewpoint and that does not count as earnings. Hence the stock has been pummeled the last few years anyway despite all the progress seen and unseen.

Latest Roadshow

David Zaslav, CEO just spent yesterday describing at a very high level yet again the work done over the last 3 1/2 years so that they could actually execute the strategy that they wanted to execute 3 1/2 years ago. The problem with this was that 3 1/2 years was too long for the market. So, Mr. Market pummeled the stock.

But shoppers, like Paramount Skydance (PSKY) know that the work that was needed was done correctly. They therefore see the value that John Malone (and likely David Zaslav) saw back when the original acquisition was made. Mr. Market clearly does not see that value. This increases the chance of an all-stock deal because people like John Malone and David Zaslav will want that gain. An all-cash deal will not do the job.

I long ago stated that people like John Malone want to triple their money in five years to account for the risk taken. Just because they have a goal similar to that does not mean they will get it. But it does mean that when the stock price went down, the individual investor had a chance to do well even if John Malone did not. It was an asymmetrical return because John Malone likely knew what he was doing far more than any of us do.

However, since Mr. Market pummeled the stock, the people behind Paramount Skydance see a far better company than was the case 3 years ago with an excellent chance of being worth far more to the market than it is now. David Zaslav, CEO detailed several parts of the company that were losing money (big time) three years ago but are now making money. That alone is a very big deal for an acquisition of that size.

Paramount Skydance can now purchase these assets for about the same stock price (or less) than it was three years ago (after all costs) while getting a far superior company ready to execute the value adding strategies that management wanted to do 3 years ago. While this management gets no credit from the market for what it has accomplished because the only outperformance in the eyes of the market was the $20 billion paydown of debt, a shopper like Paramount Skydance will give management a bit of credit (acquisition premium) and get the rest after the acquisition. This happens a whole lot with Mr. Market.

Warner Bros Discovery may benefit from an all-stock offer in that shareholders will gain as the combined company benefits from the improved prospects of Warner Bros Discovery. There may be additional benefits from combining the two companies that could add either a little or considerably to those benefits.

Financial Improvements

EBITDA growth is beginning to trend positive now that the rationalization of operations has finally concluded.

The valuation quirk is that the market cannot tell a good repair job from a bad one until there is an established earnings record. But industry professionals know a good thorough job when they see one. They are not about to wait for an established record because then the acquisition could cost their shareholders a whole lot more. Rational greed does make the market go in unexpected ways.

Meanwhile, the market was supposedly so fixated on the very important streaming segment that profitability played second fiddle to growth in subscribers. What should have been the point of concentration was the transition from big losses to the profits that have been underway for some time.

It is the same story with the cash flow and the leverage. The market got upset when cash flow declined because the minute cash flow turned positive there was a market assumption that cash flow would go "straight up". The fact that debt declined far in excess of guidance was not even on the agenda even though lower debt is very important in a leveraged buyout.

Also, the fact that the cash flow became positive as quickly as it did despite being negative at the time of the acquisition turned out to be another ignored major accomplishment by the market.

The bottom line was that for the market, the impairments and the repairs dominated the market's thoughts while a whole lot of fundamental improvements got ignored until results began showing this fiscal year. For an industry professional shopper, this is a paradise situation. It occurs more often than not.

Summary

It is all about the earnings. Paramount Skydance knows that earnings are likely to arrive for a number of reasons besides what is shown above. Mr. Market on the other hand is waiting for proof. In this case a big gain from debt retirement clouded the issue as the last article noted.

Where industry insiders see progress, the market sees ghosts in the second quarter report shown above. For industry professionals, the progress made by this management finally showed through as earnings. That likely means that the impairments are finally over with. But for the market, free cash flow went down. The market does not care that growth strategies are underway that might use cash flow in the beginning to produce benefits later because the market has been waiting for promised benefits for more than 3 years already.

Being told to wait longer just does not wash with the market. But industry insiders that understand what is going on are likely in a far better position to judge progress. Mr. Market has long expected steady and predictable growth. But the start of nearly any plan can be very bumpy with some very variable quarters until things get going.

That makes a big difference between when an acquirer might pay and what the market currently values the company. It does not mean that the company is not undervalued by the market. But it does mean that the evidence of value to the market is not currently satisfactory.

This remains a strong buy in that management has done what it needs to do. Therefore, I think a deal favorable to shareholders will result as there is still more appreciation potential here. Moreover, the rumored suitor is a bit small compared to the target. This argues for an all-stock deal that would likely benefit both companies after the combination. Now let's see what happens.\

Risk

There is always the risk that the acquiring company could be wrong to make an offer in the first place or offer the wrong combined company structure.

Warner Bros Discovery could have the wrong plan to execute or the plan could fail for reasons not currently foreseen by management.

Any acquisition can fail to meet the expectations of management. The projected improvements that resulted in an expected acquisition offer may not "pan out" for any number of reasons.

Sometimes management overestimates its ability to assimilate an acquisition. This results in setbacks because key personnel leave.

data.title: Firmer U.S. Rates Help The Dollar Steady
data.description:
Overview

After yesterday's decline following the CPI and jump in weekly jobless claims, the dollar has steadied today, even if the upticks are not so inspiring. US rates have steadied. The 10-year yield frayed the 4% threshold for the first time in five months but settled a little above it. Today it is a couple of basis points firmer around 4.04%. It is flat on the week. In the firmer US dollar environment today, the Canadian dollar is faring best, off less than 0.1%. The yen is the weakest of the G10 currencies, down about 0.5%, and the dollar is knocking on JPY148 (yesterday's high was around JPY148.15). Emerging market currencies are mixed. For the week, the JPMorgan emerging market currency index is up marginally for the first time in three weeks, while the MSCI emerging market currency index is up about 0.35% for its third consecutive weekly advance.

The S&P 500 and Nasdaq have four-day advances on the line and are at record levels. The index futures are trading slightly softer. In the Asia Pacific region, nearly all the markets but China rallied. The Nikkei is at new highs, while Hong Kong, South Korea, Taiwan, and a couple of smaller markets rallied more than 1% today. However, Europe's Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is nursing a small loss, but it is up about 1% this week, its first weekly advance in three. Benchmark 10-year yields are 2-4 bp higher in Europe, and the rates are setting new highs for the week. A French downgrade by Fitch later today is still a reasonable possibility, though its premium over Germany is little changed on the week after yesterday's and today's slight narrowing. Gold pulled back slightly (~0.2%) yesterday but has recovered today. The record-high was set Tuesday near $3675, and it is around $3645 now. October WTI approached last week's low (~$61.45) earlier today but found bids that lifted it back toward $62.85. It settled last week slightly below $62.00.

USD: The Dollar Index posted a bearish outside down day by trading on both sides of Wednesday's range and settled below its low, but there has been no follow-through selling. It is little changed on the week. Last Friday, after the employment report, DXY settled near 97.77. The busy week ends quietly with the preliminary September University of Michigan survey. Bloomberg's survey expected a small decline in sentiment, but a slight improvement in the assessment of current conditions and expectations. Inflation expectations are expected to have softened. With preliminary benchmark job growth and the PPI and CPI behind us, the focus is squarely on next week's FOMC meeting. The implied year-end effective Fed funds rate has fallen from almost 3.75% before the August jobs report to about 3.60% after yesterday's CPI and the unexpected strong rise in weekly jobless claims to their highest level in four years (distorted, apparently by the Texas flood and perhaps the timing of the Labor Day holiday). Assuming Miran's nomination is confirmed, there is some speculation that he may dissent in favor of a 50 bp move from what seems to be a consensus for a quarter-point cut. It may play on concerns about the Fed's independence, especially if the president can secure a majority of the Board of Governors. The Justice Department is seeking an appeals court decision by Monday to stay the decision that is allowing Governor Cook to remain in office while her legal appeal runs its course. There is increased recognition that a majority of governors could also influence the selection of the regional Fed presidents, who need to be reconfirmed by next March. Recall that both Governors Waller and Bowman abstained in the board's confirmation of Chicago Fed President Goolsbee.

EURO: The euro posted a bullish outside up day yesterday. It edged a couple of hundredths of a cent higher today to almost $1.1750 before sellers emerged and pushed it back below last week's settlement slightly below $1.1715. If it closes above there today, it will be the fifth weekly advance in the past six weeks. Although the French 10-year premium over Germany is virtually unchanged compared with the end of last week and the week before (~ <80 bp), there is risk that Fitch will downgrade France later today from its current AA- status. Fitch is also reviewing Portugal's A- rating, but it has a positive outlook. S&P upgraded Portugal last month. Hardly a surprise, the ECB maintained a steady policy yesterday. If its easing cycle is not done, it is one cut away. The swaps market has about 10 bp or about a 40% chance of another cut next year, discounted. The staff forecast adjusted lifted this year and next year's inflation forecasts and shaved 2027 projection. This year's CPI is now seen at 2.1% (from 2.0%). Next year, it is projected to be 1.7% (from 1.6%). The CPI forecast for 2027 was shaved to 1.9% (from 2.0%). This year's growth was revised to 1.2% from 0.9%, which seems optimistic, while next year's GDP forecast was pared to 1% (1.1% previously). The 2027 GDP was left unchanged at 1.3%.

CNY: The dollar had forged a near-term shelf near CNH7.1135. The broad setback in the dollar yesterday pushed the greenback marginally lower to CNH7.1120, almost after it reached the CNH7.1260 area. It is trading inside yesterday's range today. The PBOC has continued to guide the dollar lower. On a weekly basis, the fix has only risen in three weeks since mid-May. The PBOC set the dollar fix at CNY7.1019 (CNY7.1034 yesterday). Some observers believe the PBOC is gradually moving toward 7.0%. While that is the direction, the target seems to be a matter of speculation rather than a signal from officials. Separately, from May's low, the CSI 300 rallied almost 30%. August lending figures were released, and new yuan loans and aggregate financing improved sequentially. In the year-to-date, through August, aggregate financing is up about 21% from August 2024.

JPY: The dollar reversed lower against the yen yesterday. It first rose to a three-day, slightly above JPY148.15. It reversed lower to straddle the JPY147 area in the North American afternoon, which coincides with the (61.8%) retracement of the rally from Tuesday's low around JPY146.30. The dollar recovered today and has returned to the JPY148 area. Since August 1, the dollar has not settled below JPY146.90 or above JPY148.80. Japan reported its final July industrial output earlier today, trimming the initial 1.6% decline to -1.2%, which reversed most a little more than half of the 2.1% gain in June. On average, it rose by 0.2% a month in the Jan-July period. In the first seven months of last year, it averaged a 0.3% decline. Next week, Japan reports August trade balance. It is likely to be in deficit for the fifth month this year. It also reports August CPI, but the signal from the Tokyo figures out a few weeks ago points to a softening toward 2.8% (from 3.1%), which would be lowest since last October. The BOJ meets next week, but it is highly unlikely to do anything. The swaps market has about 15.5 bp of a hike discounted for this year, up roughly 3.5 bp this week but still slightly less than at the end of August.

GBP: Sterling posted an outside upside yesterday. It initially slipped to a three-day low near $1.3495 but was lifted by the broad dollar sell-off to almost $1.3585. Tuesday's high was about $1.3590, and the high from the second half of July and again near mid-August was almost $1.3600. Barring a settlement above there today, it will be the fifth consecutive week that sterling finished with a $1.35-handle. Sterling has not traded above $1.3600 since mid-July. The UK's economy stagnated in July after growing by 0.4% in June. Industrial tumbled an expected 0.9% while services eked out a 0.1% gain. Construction surprisingly rose 0.2%, but the trade deficit unexpectedly rose. Weak growth exacerbates Chancellor Reeve's fiscal challenges. Before the Bank of England meets on September 18, the UK will provide an update of the labor market, which has been slowing, and August CPI. The UK has the highest consumer inflation in the G10 at 3.8% in July. There is practically no chance of a change in policy by the BOE. The swaps market has a little less than a 40% chance of cut discounted for the end of the year. Back in late April/early May, the swaps market implied a year-end rate of about 3.50%. Now it is slightly above 3.87%.

CAD: The greenback rose to CAD1.3890 yesterday, its best level since August 22 and Powell's speech at Jackson Hole. It reversed and was sold for almost CAD1.3825. This met the (38.2%) retracement of the rally from the August 29 low near CAD1.3725. The US dollar did not close below Wednesday's low (~CAD1.3830), which may minimize the damage for technical purists. It settled at CAD1.3830 last week. The greenback is firmed but holding below CAD1.3850 so far today. Canada’s July building permits and Q2 capacity utilization rate on tap for today are hardly the stuff that moves the exchange rate. The key is still the overall US dollar direction. The Bank of Canada meets a few hours before the FOMC meets, and it will likely deliver another 25 bp rate cut. It cut twice in Q1 but has been steady since, with its overnight lending rate at 2.75%. Disappointing Q2 GDP and August jobs data spurred the speculation of a rate cut. The August CPI will be reported the day before the Bank of Canada meets. The swaps market has about a 65% chance of another cut before the end of the year.

AUD: The Australian dollar extended its advance to a new high for the year near $0.6665 yesterday and edged a little higher to almost $0.6670 today before steadying. It has risen by about 3.7% since before Fed Chair Powell spoke at Jackson Hole on August 22. It is the strongest currency in the G10 over this period. It also outperformed all of the emerging market currencies as well. Some might link it to the strong rally in Chinese equities. The rolling 30-day correlation of differences is near 0.40 now, up from less than 0.10 on August 21, and there was an inverse correlation from late May through late June. Initial support is seen around $0.6625. This is the third consecutive weekly gain for the Aussie, its long streak since April-May. Next week's data highlight is the August jobs report on September 18. The 60.5k increase in full-time jobs was the most since February 2024, and since then, implied year-end rate in the futures market has risen by around a dozen basis points to 3.30%.

MXN: Mexico's industrial output tumbled by 1.2% in July (median forecast in Bloomberg's survey was for a 0.2% decline) and adding insult to injury, June's 0.1% decline was revised to -0.3%. This would seem to boost confidence that Banxico will cut rates again at the end of the month. The weaker dollar, however, was a more important driver of the exchange rate and it was sold to a new low for the year near MXN18.4525. A convincing break could see MXN18.40 next. The greenback steadied today but met resistance slightly above MXN18.50.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: 3 Dividend Stocks That Pay Me $2,000 Each Month
data.description:
Generating significant monthly passive income from dividend stocks (SCHD) is a great way to set yourself up for financial independence and early retirement. If you have steady cash flow coming in that meets your living expenses, then you do not need to work a job to support yourself. This gives you the freedom to choose whether you want to work at all, or at the very least, the liberty to take a lower-paying but more fulfilling job rather than chasing a salary for a role you do not like or one that comes with a stressful commute and work schedule.

When building a passive income stream with the goal of generating strong monthly cash flow, you can take one of two approaches. One option is to invest in monthly-paying dividend stocks and funds, some of the most popular of which are triple-net lease REITs like Realty Income (O) that pay monthly dividends, or popular covered call ETFs like JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) that pay attractive monthly distributions. The other option is to strategically purchase quarterly-paying dividend stocks, staggering them based on their payout schedules to generate fairly consistent monthly dividend income.

In this article, I am going to share three high-conviction and high-quality dividend stocks that each pay their quarterly dividends on different schedules. I also happen to own a lot of equity in them, and together they pay me about $2,000 in passive income every month.

January, April, July, October

The first holding to discuss is Enterprise Products Partners (EPD). EPD is the quintessential retirement and passive income investment in my view because it scores extremely high on all the key metrics that I look at.

First and foremost, it pays out a very attractive yield of 7% on a next-12-month basis. Additionally, that distribution yield is tax-advantaged, given that it is a K-1–issuing MLP and therefore its distributions are largely classified as return of capital. It also has a very strong balance sheet with the best credit rating in the midstream space at A- from S&P, while maintaining a low 3.1x leverage ratio, a weighted average maturity on its debt of about 18 years, significant liquidity, and strong free cash flow generation. Its balance sheet is highly defensive and durable, as it has consistently posted double-digit percentage returns on invested capital year after year, even during periods of significant macroeconomic or energy price weakness.

EPD’s distributable cash flow per unit has consistently grown over time, fueling distribution growth per unit every year for over a quarter century. Moreover, its long-duration contracted cash flows—which make up the vast majority of its EBITDA—are backed by a well-diversified portfolio across virtually every midstream segment. Insider alignment is also strong, with management and insiders owning about a third of EPD’s equity. On top of this, the distribution is very safe, as it is covered about 1.7x by distributable cash flow.

Looking forward, in the next year or two, EPD will have a significant amount of growth projects come online, which will drive a large increase in EBITDA while also materially reducing its leverage, thanks to both the boost in earnings and the decline in capex once these projects are completed. This will create significant capacity to unlock further value for investors through a mix of potential unit repurchases, accelerated distribution growth, or even opportunistic acquisitions.

February, May, August, November

The next income holding I am going to talk about today is Western Midstream Partners (WES). Like EPD, it is a K-1–issuing midstream infrastructure business (AMLP) with tax-advantaged distributions. However, it offers an even higher distribution yield of 9.6% on a next-12-month basis. Like EPD, it is also highly likely to continue growing its distribution year after year, with both expected to grow at an inflation-beating mid-single-digit CAGR moving forward. This means that in addition to their already high yields, you should be able to count on those payouts increasing at a pace that at least keeps up with, and likely exceeds, inflation.

Moreover, WES typically pays its distributions in February, May, August, and November, which complements EPD’s differing payout schedule quite nicely. Beyond the timing, WES has a lot going for it. It maintains an attractive investment-grade balance sheet with a leverage ratio around 3x, well-laddered debt maturities, and significant liquidity.

Additionally, WES recently completed a major acquisition of Aris Water Solutions, which not only provides significant organic growth opportunities moving forward but is also immediately accretive to free cash flow. The synergies from this acquisition are expected to be substantial and relatively easy to realize, as they come primarily from G&A efficiencies gained by combining the two companies.

When you combine WES’s sky-high yield—well covered by free cash flow—with its strong balance sheet, very stable cash flow profile supported by long-duration, volume-based, commodity-price-resistant contracts, and its bolt-on acquisition strategy, the partnership looks well-positioned to continue paying a very lucrative quarterly paycheck. Additionally, it should deliver growth that not only keeps up with but likely beats inflation over time.

March, June, September, December

The third high-yield stock that pays me $2,000 a month during March, June, September, and December is Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (MSDL). It is a business development company (BIZD) that issues a 1099 tax form, with dividends almost always taxed as ordinary income.
